question: Based on this image, what is the injury severity category?
ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the axial contrast-enhanced CT image focusing on the kidneys. I see the left kidney has a clear linear hypodense defect extending from the cortical surface into the renal parenchyma (red arrow), with an adjacent crescentic collection consistent with a subcapsular hematoma. The right kidney demonstrates symmetric contrast enhancement and no acute abnormality.

Next, I relate these observations to the question, which asks for the injury severity category. I consider the key imaging features visible here: a distinct parenchymal laceration that extends beyond the cortex into the kidney substance, presence of a subcapsular hematoma, and no obvious contrast extravasation into the collecting system, no gross fragmentation/shattered appearance of the kidney, and no large devascularized segment or vascular pedicle injury evident on this slice.

Then, I compare those imaging features against the available severity categories. The pattern—single deep parenchymal laceration with subcapsular hematoma but without collecting-system rupture or major vascular injury—aligns best with the moderate injury category.

Therefore, based on the image findings, the appropriate choice is the moderate injury category (AAST Grade III).
caption: CT and renal artery angiography images: a Abdominopelvic non-contrast CT demonstrated abnormal left kidney contour that was interiorly displaced and externally compressed. A large but limited subcapsular collection with mixed density lateral to the left kidney was seen, with an overall size of 5.8 × 10.1 × 11.7 cm, red arrow; b Contrast-enhanced abdominopelvic CT showed renal parenchymal laceration> 1 cm in depth without collecting system rupture or urinary extravasation. The subcapsular hematoma was shrunk than before with an overall left kidney size of about 4.7 × 8.9 × 11.7 cm, red arrow. In contrast, the right kidney filled uniformly with intravenous dye and showed no obvious sign of injury; c The left kidney was significantly deformed due to suppression. Renal arteriography identified active contrast extravasation from a distant branch of the left renal artery, red arrow; d Post-embolization film showed successful obliteration of bleeding branch, red arrow
sub_caption: b Contrast-enhanced abdominopelvic CT showed renal parenchymal laceration> 1 cm in depth without collecting system rupture or urinary extravasation. The subcapsular hematoma was shrunk than before with an overall left kidney size of about 4.7 × 8.9 × 11.7 cm, red arrow. In contrast, the right kidney filled uniformly with intravenous dye and showed no obvious sign of injury;
image_context:
On admission, the patient was restless but responded correctly. Physical examination revealed pallor and relatively unstable vital signs (blood pressure, 149/85 mmHg; pulse, 130 beats/min, T 36.5 °C). Inspection revealed scattered ecchymosis due to anticoagulant injections on the abdomen. Abdominal palpation showed involuntary guarding and rigidity. Left flank tenderness on deep palpation was noted. Due to general weakness and frailty, it is hard for the patient to recall when the pain started creeping up. Initial investigations revealed a hemoglobin level of 42 g/L. Serum creatinine peaked at 296.2 μmol/L. Coagulation test was relatively normal except for high d-dimer level of 1.25 mg/L (normal range < 0.24 mg/L). Bedside abdominal ultrasonography showed a large left peri-renal fluid collection. There was no history of abdominal trauma. Therefore, spontaneous perirenal hemorrhage was highly suspected. A subsequent non-contrast abdominopelvic CT scan demonstrated subcapsular and perirenal hematoma of the left kidney (Fig. <xref rid="12882_2021_2424_Fig1_HTML" ref-type="fig">1</xref>a). Emergency renal arteriography identified active extravasation of contrast media from a distant branch of the left renal artery (Fig. a). Emergency renal arteriography identified active extravasation of contrast media from a distant branch of the left renal artery (Fig. <xref rid="12882_2021_2424_Fig1_HTML" ref-type="fig">1</xref>c). Furthermore, selective transcatheter embolization effectively obliterated the bleeding spot (Fig. c). Furthermore, selective transcatheter embolization effectively obliterated the bleeding spot (Fig. <xref rid="12882_2021_2424_Fig1_HTML" ref-type="fig">1</xref>d). With red blood cell transfusion and closely monitoring of hemoglobin, there was no sign of active bleeding. As the bleeding stopped, the patient continued 40 mg of methylprednisolone daily since admission for major organ involvement. Four days after admission, contrast-enhanced abdominopelvic CT confirmed renal parenchymal laceration (Fig. d). With red blood cell transfusion and closely monitoring of hemoglobin, there was no sign of active bleeding. As the bleeding stopped, the patient continued 40 mg of methylprednisolone daily since admission for major organ involvement. Four days after admission, contrast-enhanced abdominopelvic CT confirmed renal parenchymal laceration (Fig. <xref rid="12882_2021_2424_Fig1_HTML" ref-type="fig">1</xref>b).
b).

A large but limited subcapsular collection with mixed density lateral to the left kidney was seen, with an overall size of 5.8 × 10.1 × 11.7 cm, red arrow; b Contrast-enhanced abdominopelvic CT showed renal parenchymal laceration> 1 cm in depth without collecting system rupture or urinary extravasation. The subcapsular hematoma was shrunk than before with an overall left kidney size of about 4.7 × 8.9 × 11.7 cm, red arrow. In contrast, the right kidney filled uniformly with intravenous dye and showed no obvious sign of injury; c The left kidney was significantly deformed due to suppression. Renal arteriography identified active contrast extravasation from a distant branch of the left renal artery, red arrow; d Post-embolization film showed successful obliteration of bleeding branch, red arrow
relevant_image_context: Four days after admission, contrast-enhanced abdominopelvic CT confirmed renal parenchymal laceration (Fig. 1b). Contrast-enhanced abdominopelvic CT showed renal parenchymal laceration >1 cm in depth without collecting system rupture or urinary extravasation. The subcapsular hematoma was shrunk from before with an overall left kidney size of about 4.7 × 8.9 × 11.7 cm, red arrow. In contrast, the right kidney filled uniformly with intravenous dye and showed no obvious sign of injury.
